params.text:
What the law expects

The Health and Safety at Work etc. Act 1974 places a general duty on your employer to protect the health, safety and welfare of employees, so far as is reasonably practicable. The Management of Health and Safety at Work Regulations 1999 build on this, requiring employers to assess the risks that lone workers face and to put sensible controls in place.

The Care Quality Commission (CQC) expects providers to keep people who use services and their staff safe, and the Social Care Institute for Excellence (SCIE) promotes safe systems of work as part of good practice. Together these mean lone working must be planned, not left to chance.

Shared responsibility

Safety is a two-way street. Your employer sets up safe systems; you follow them and speak up when something is not right.

  • Employers must assess the risks, provide training and equipment, set up ways to keep in touch, and respond if something goes wrong.
  • Employees must take reasonable care of themselves and others, follow the agreed procedures, use the equipment provided, and report concerns and incidents.

params.question: Under the Health and Safety at Work Act 1974, who is mainly responsible for assessing the risks of lone working?

answers.text: The employer, who must assess and control the risks so far as is reasonably practicable

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Correct. The Act places the primary duty on the employer to assess and control risks, while employees follow the agreed safe systems.

answers.text: The individual care worker, entirely on their own

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: No. Employees have duties too, but the main legal duty to assess and control the risks sits with the employer.
No. The person you support is not responsible for your workplace risk assessment. That duty belongs to your employer.

answers.text: Nobody, because there is no law that applies to lone working

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: No. There is no law banning lone working, but the Health and Safety at Work Act clearly applies and places duties on the employer.
